extra.message: User input from query parameter 'username' is directly concatenated into SQL query string passed to run_sql_query(). The endpoint accepts username as a string parameter and inserts it directly into an f-string SQL query without any parameterization or escaping. This allows SQL injection attacks.

User-supplied data from User model fields (name, username, password, address, email, contact) are directly concatenated into SQL INSERT query. The f-string at line 188-204 embeds user.name, user.username, user.address, user.email, and user.contact directly without parameterization, allowing SQL injection attacks.

User input from 'username' query parameter is directly concatenated into SQL DELETE query. The f-string at line 231 inserts the username parameter directly into the SQL statement without any parameterization or escaping, allowing SQL injection attacks.

User-supplied data from User model field 'address' is directly concatenated into SQL DELETE query. The f-string at line 235 inserts user.address directly without any parameterization, allowing SQL injection attacks.

Database initialization code uses f-string to build SQL INSERT queries with Faker-generated values (name, username, password, address, email, contact) concatenated directly without escaping or parameterization. While values are from Faker library in this context, the pattern is vulnerable and could be exploited if initialization logic is modified. This demonstrates insecure SQL query construction.

The app.title variable is concatenated directly into HTML response without escaping. If app.title were controllable by user input or derived from untrusted sources, this could lead to HTML injection or XSS. Currently hardcoded as 'vFastAPI', but the pattern is insecure.

The app.title variable is concatenated directly into HTML response without escaping. If app.title were controllable by user input or derived from untrusted sources, this could lead to HTML injection or XSS. Currently hardcoded as 'vFastAPI', but the pattern is insecure.

The /find POST endpoint accepts arbitrary JSON query objects and passes them directly to get_nosql_users() which forwards them to MongoDB find() method. While MontyDB is a Python implementation of MongoDB, the find() method can be exploited with operator injection (e.g., {"username": {"$ne": null}}) to bypass authentication or retrieve unintended data.
